title: Rotting Oranges
description: You are given an m x n grid where each cell can have one of three values: 0 representing an empty cell, 1 representing a fresh orange, or 2 representing a rotten orange. Every minute, any fresh orange that is 4-directionally adjacent to a rotten orange becomes rotten. Return the minimum number of minutes that must elapse until no cell has a fresh orange. If this is impossible, return -1.
thinking_questions: All rotten oranges spread simultaneously each minute. What traversal models simultaneous spreading?
How do you handle multiple starting points (multiple rotten oranges)?
After BFS completes, how do you check if any fresh orange remains?

hints.L1: Multi-source BFS from all initially rotten oranges simultaneously. Each BFS level = one minute.
hints.L2: Add all rotten oranges to queue. BFS level by level. Count fresh oranges. Decrement as they rot. If fresh > 0 at end, return -1.
hints.L3:
# INSIGHT: multi-source BFS — all rotten spread simultaneously
from collections import deque
q = deque()
fresh = 0
for r in range(rows):
    for c in range(cols):
        if grid[r][c] == 2: q.append((r,c,0))  # rotten + time
        elif grid[r][c] == 1: fresh += 1
time = 0
while q:
    r, c, t = q.popleft()
    for dr, dc in [(0,1),(0,-1),(1,0),(-1,0)]:
        nr, nc = r+dr, c+dc
        if 0<=nr<rows and 0<=nc<cols and grid[nr][nc] == 1:
            grid[nr][nc] = 2; fresh -= 1
            q.append((nr,nc,t+1)); time = t+1
return time if fresh == 0 else -1
# Time: O(m·n)  Space: O(m·n)

follow_up_questions: Why is BFS better than DFS for this problem?
How does multi-source BFS differ from single-source BFS?
What if rotting could spread diagonally too?
solution_notes: Multi-source BFS from all rotten cells. Track fresh count. Return -1 if fresh > 0 after BFS. O(m·n).
